title: ONLINE MAP INFERENCE AND LEARNING FOR NONSYMMETRIC DETERMINANTAL POINT PROCESSES [PDF]

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## ONLINE MAP INFERENCE AND LEARNING FOR NONSYMMETRIC DETERMINANTAL POINT PROCESSES

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In this paper, we introduce the online and streaming MAP inference and learning problems for Non-symmetric Determinantal Point Processes (NDPPs) where data points arrive in an arbitrary order and the algorithms are constrained to use a singlepass over the data as well as sub-linear memory. The online setting has an additional requirement of maintaining a valid solution at any point in time. For solving these new problems, we propose algorithms with theoretical guarantees, evaluate them on several real-world datasets, and show that they give comparable performance to state-of-the-art offline algorithms that store the entire data in memory and take multiple passes over it.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Determinantal Point Processes (DPPs) were first introduced in the context of quantum mechanics (Macchi, 1975) and have subsequently been extensively studied with applications in several areas of pure and applied mathematics like graph theory, combinatorics, random matrix theory (Hough et al., 2006; Borodin, 2009), and randomized numerical linear algebra (Derezinski & Mahoney, 2021). Discrete DPPs have gained widespread adoption in machine learning following the seminal work of Kulesza & Taskar (2012) and there has been a recent explosion of interest in DPPs in the machine learning community. For instance, some of the very recent uses of DPPs include automation of deep neural network design (Nguyen et al., 2021), deep generative models (Chen & Ahmed, 2021), document and video summarization (Perez-Beltrachini & Lapata, 2021), image processing (Launay et al., 2021), and learning in games (Perez-Nieves et al., 2021).

A DPP is a probability distribution over subsets of items and is characterized by some kernel matrix such that the probability of sampling any particular subset is proportional to the determinant of the submatrix corresponding to that subset in the kernel. Until very recently, most prior work on DPPs focused on the setting where the kernel matrix is symmetric. Due to this constraint, DPPs can only model negative correlations between items. Recent work has shown that allowing the kernel matrix to be nonsymmetric can greatly increase the expressive power of DPPs and allows them to model _compatible_ sets of items (Gartrell et al., 2019; Brunel, 2018). To differentiate this line of work from prior literature on symmetric DPPs, the term Nonsymmetric DPPs (NDPPs) has often been used. Modeling positive correlations can be useful in many practical scenarios. For instance, an E-commerce company trying to build a product recommendation system would want the system to increase the probability of suggesting a router if a customer adds a modem to a shopping cart.

State-of-the-art algorithms for learning and inference on NDPPs (Gartrell et al., 2021) require storing the full data in memory and take multiple passes over the complete dataset. Therefore, these algorithms take too much memory to be useful for large scale data, where the size of the entire dataset can be much larger than the random-access memory available. These algorithms are also not practical in settings where data is generated on the fly, for example, in E-commerce applications where new items are added to the store over time, and more importantly, added to the carts of users instantaneously.

This work makes the following contributions:

**Streaming and Online Inference:** We formulate streaming and online versions of maximum a posteriori (MAP) inference on fixed-size NDPPs and provide algorithms for solving these problems. In the streaming setting, data points arrive in an arbitrary order and the algorithms are constrained to

use a single-pass over the data as well as sub-linear memory (i.e. memory that is substantially smaller than the size of the data stream). The online setting we consider has an additional restriction that we need to maintain a valid solution at every time step. For both these settings, we provide algorithms which have comparable or even better solution quality than the offline greedy algorithm while taking only a single pass over the data and using a fraction of the memory used by the offline algorithm.

**Online Learning:** We introduce the online learning problem for NDPPs and provide an algorithm which solves this problem using a single-pass over the data and memory that is constant in _m_ , the number of baskets in the training data (or equivalently the length of the stream). In comparison, the offline learning algorithm takes a large number of passes over the entire data and uses memory linear in _m_ . Strikingly, our online learning algorithm shows comparable performance (log-likelihood) to the state-of-the-art offline learning algorithm, while converging significantly faster in all cases (Figure 2). This is notable, since our algorithm uses only a single pass over the data, while using a tiny fraction of the memory.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Even in the case of (symmetric) DPPs, the study of online and streaming settings is in a nascent stage. In particular, Bhaskara et al. (2020) were the first to propose online algorithms for MAP inference of DPPs and Liu et al. (2021) were the first to give streaming algorithms for the maximum induced cardinality objective proposed by Gillenwater et al. (2018). However, no work has focused on either online or streaming MAP inference or online learning for Nonsymmetric DPPs.

A special subset of NDPPs called signed DPPs were the first class of NDPPs to be studied (Brunel et al., 2017). Gartrell et al. (2019) studied a more general class of NDPPs and provided learning and MAP Inference algorithms, and also showed that NDPPs have additional expressiveness over symmetric DPPs and can better model certain problems. This was improved by Gartrell et al. (2021) in which they provided a new decomposition which enabled linear time learning and MAP Inference for NDPPs. More recently, Anari & Vuong (2021) proposed the first algorithm with a _k[O]_[(] _[k]_[)] approximation factor for MAP Inference on NDPPs where _k_ is the number of items to be selected. These works are not amenable to the streaming nor online settings that are studied in our paper. In particular, they store all data in memory and use multiple passes over the data, among other issues. In this work, we formally introduce the streaming and online MAP inference and online learning problems for NDPPs and develop online algorithms for solving these problems. To the best of our knowledge, our work is the first to study NDPPs in the streaming and online settings, and develop algorithms for solving MAP inference and learning of NDPPs in these settings.

## 3 PRELIMINARIES

**Notation.** Throughout the paper, we use uppercase bold letters ( _**A**_ ) to denote matrices and lowercase bold letters ( _**a**_ ) to denote vectors. Letters in normal font ( _a_ ) will be used for scalars. For any positive integer _n_ , we use [ _n_ ] to denote the set _{_ 1 _,_ 2 _, . . . , n}_ . A matrix _**M**_ is said to be skew-symmetric if _**M**_ = _−_ _**M**[⊤]_ where _⊤_ is used to represent matrix transposition.

A DPP is a probability distribution on all subsets of [ _n_ ] characterized by a matrix _**L** ∈_ R _[n][×][n]_ . The probability of sampling any subset _S ⊆_ [ _n_ ] i.e. Pr[ _S_ ] _∝_ det( _**L** S_ ) where _**L** S_ is the submatrix of _**L**_

Therefore, Pr[ _S_ ] = det( _**L**_ + _**I** n_ )[.][For the DPP] corresponding to _**L**_ to be a valid probability distribution, we need det( _**L** S_ ) _≥_ 0 for all _S ⊆_ [ _n_ ] since Pr[ _S_ ] _≥_ 0 for all _S ⊆_ [ _n_ ]. Matrices which satisfy this property are known as _P_ 0-matrices (Fiedler & Pták, 1966). For any symmetric matrix _**L**_ , det( _**L** S_ ) _≥_ 0 for all _S ⊆_ [ _n_ ] if and only if _**L**_ is positive semi-definite (PSD) i.e. _**x**[T]_ _**Lx** ≥_ 0 for all _**x** ∈_ R _[n]_ . Therefore, all symmetric matrices which correspond to valid DPPs are PSD. But there are _P_ 0-matrices which are not necessarily symmetric 1 1 (or even positive semi-definite). For example, _**L**_ = _−_ 1 1 is a nonsymmetric _P_ 0 matrix. � �

Any matrix _**L**_ can be uniquely written as the sum of a symmetric and skew-symmetric matrix: _**L**_ = ( _**L**_ + _**L**[⊤]_ ) _/_ 2 + ( _**L** −_ _**L**[⊤]_ ) _/_ 2. For the DPP characterized by _**L**_ , the symmetric part of the decomposition can be thought of as encoding negative correlations between items and the skewsymmetric part as encoding positive correlations. Gartrell et al. (2019) proposed a decomposition which covers the set of all nonsymmetric PSD matrices (a subset of _P_ 0 matrices) which allowed them to provide a cubic time algorithm (in the ground set size) for NDPP learning. This decomposition is _**L**_ = _**V**[⊤]_ _**V**_ +( _**BC**[⊤] −_ _**CB**[⊤]_ ). Gartrell et al. (2021) provided more efficient (linear time) algorithms for learning and MAP inference using a new decomposition _**L**_ = _**V**[⊤]_ _**V**_ + _**B**[⊤]_ _**CB**_ . Although both these decompositions only cover a subset of _P_ 0 matrices, it turns out that they are quite useful for modeling real world instances and provide improved results when compared to (symmetric) DPPs.

For the decomposition _**L**_ = _**V**[⊤]_ _**V**_ + _**B**[⊤]_ _**CB**_ , we have _**V** ,_ _**B** ∈_ R _[d][×][n] ,_ _**C** ∈_ R _[d][×][d]_ and _**C**_ is skewsymmetric. Here we can think of the items having having a latent low-dimensional representation ( _**v** i,_ _**b** i_ ) where _**v** i,_ _**b** i ∈_ R _[d]_ . Intuitively, a low-dimensional representation (when compared to _n_ ) is sufficient for representing items because any particular item only interacts with a small number of other items in real-world datasets, as evidenced by the fact that the maximum basket size encountered in real-world data is much smaller than _n_ .

## 4 STREAMING MAP INFERENCE

In this section, we formulate the streaming MAP inference problem for NDPPs and design an algorithm for this problem with guarantees on the solution quality, space, and time.

## 4.1 STREAMING MAP INFERENCE PROBLEM

We study the MAP Inference problem in low-rank NDPPs in the streaming setting where we see columns of a 2 _d × n_ matrix in order (column-arrival model). Given some fixed skew-symmetric matrix _C ∈_ R _[d][×][d]_ , consider a stream of 2 _d_ -dimensional vectors (which can be viewed as pairs of _d_ -dimensional vectors) arriving in order:

The main goal in the streaming setting is to output the maximum likelihood subset _S ⊆_ [ _n_ ] of cardinality _k_ at the end of the stream assuming that _S_ is drawn from the NDPP characterized by _**L**_ = _**V**[⊤]_ _**V**_ + _**B**[⊤]_ _**CB**_ i.e.

For any _S ⊆_ [ _n_ ] _,_ _**V** S ∈_ R _[d][×|][S][|]_ is the matrix whose each column corresponds to _{_ _**v** i, i ∈ S}_ . Similarly, _**B** S ∈_ R _[d][×|][S][|]_ is the matrix whose columns correspond to _{_ _**b** i, i ∈ S}_ . In the case of symmetric DPPs, this maximization problem in the non-streaming setting corresponds to MAP Inference in cardinality constrained DPPs, also known as _k_ -DPPs (Kulesza & Taskar, 2011).

Usually, designing a streaming algorithm can be viewed as a dynamic data-structure design problem. We want to maintain a data-structure with efficient time and small space over the entire stream. Therefore, the secondary goals are to minimize the following:

- Space: We consider word-RAM model, and use number of words[*] to measure it.

## 5 ONLINE MAP INFERENCE FOR NDPPS

We now consider the online MAP inference problem for NDPPs, which is natural in settings where data is generated on the fly. In addition to the constraints of the streaming setting (Section 4.1), our online setting requires us to maintain a valid solution at every time step. In this section, we provide two algorithms for solving this problem.

## 5.1 ONLINE LOCAL SEARCH WITH A STASH

**Outline of Algorithm 2** : On a high-level, our algorithm is a generalization of the Online-LS algorithm for DPPs from Bhaskara et al. (2020). At each time step _t ∈_ [ _n_ ] (after _t ≥ k_ ), our algorithm maintains a candidate solution subset of indices _S_ of cardinality _k_ from the data seen so far i.e. _S ⊆_ [ _t_ ] s.t. _|S|_ = _k_ in a streaming fashion. Additionally, it also maintains two matrices _**V** S,_ _**B** S ∈_ R _[d][×|][S][|]_ where the columns of _**V** S_ are _{_ _**v** i, i ∈ S}_ and the columns of _**B** S_ are _{_ _**b** i, i ∈ S}_ . Whenever the algorithm sees a new data point ( _**v** t,_ _**b** t_ ), it replaces an existing index from _S_ with the newly arrived index if doing so increases _f_ ( _S_ ) at-least by a factor of _α ≥_ 1 where _α_ is a parameter to be chosen (we can think of _α_ being 2 for understanding the algorithm). Instead of just deleting the index replaced from _S_ , it is stored in an auxiliary set _T_ called the “stash" (and also maintains corresponding matrices _**V** T ,_ _**B** T_ ), which the algorithm then uses to performs a local search over to find a locally optimal solution.
We now define a data-dependent parameter ∆ which we will need to describe the time and space used by Algorithm 2.

**Outline of Algorithm 3** : Similar to Algorithm 2, our new algorithm also maintains two subsets of indices _S_ and _T_ , and corresponding data matrices _**V** S,_ _**B** S,_ _**V** T ,_ _**B** T_ . Whenever the algorithm sees a new data-point ( _**v** t,_ _**b** t_ ), it checks if the solution quality _f_ ( _S_ ) can be improved by a factor of _α_ by replacing any element in _S_ with the newly seen data-point. Additionally, it also checks if the solution quality can be made better by including both the points ( _**v** t,_ _**b** t_ ) and the data-point ( _**v** t−_ 1 _,_ _**b** t−_ 1). Further, the algorithm tries to improve the solution quality by performing a local search on _N_ 2( _S, T_ ) i.e. the neighborhood of the candidate solution _S_ using the stash _T_ by replacing at most two elements of _S_ . There might be interactions captured by _pairs_ of items which are much stronger than single items in NDPPs (see example 5 from Anari & Vuong (2021)).

## 6 ONLINE LEARNING

In this section, we will first formally introduce the online learning problem for NDPPs and then develop an online algorithm for this new setting with theoretical guarantees. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to study the online learning setting for NDPPs.

## 6.1 ONLINE SETTING

The online learning problem for NDPPs is formally defined as follows:

**Definition 8** (Online NDPP Learning) **.** _Given a continuous stream of observed sets {S_ 1 _, . . . , St, . . . , } of items from_ [ _n_ ] _, the online learning problem is to maintain an NDPP kernel_

## 7 EXPERIMENTS

Our experiments are designed to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed online inference and learning algorithms for NDPPs. Experiments are performed using a standard desktop computer (Quad-Core Intel Core i7, 16 GB RAM) using many real-world datasets. Details of the datasets can be found in Appendix E.

## 7.1 ONLINE INFERENCE

As a point of comparison, we also use the state-of-the-art offline greedy algorithm from Gartrell et al. (2021). This algorithm stores all data in memory and makes _k_ passes over the dataset and in each round, picks the data point which gives the maximum marginal gain in solution value. Online-Greedy replaces a point in the current solution set with the observed point if doing so increases the objective. See Algorithm 5 in Appendix B for more details.

We first learn all the matrices _**B** ,_ _**C** ,_ and _**V**_ by applying the offline learning algorithm. The learning algorithm takes as input a parameter _d_ , which is the embedding size for _**V**_ , _**B**_ , _**C**_ . We use _d_ = 10 for all datasets other than Instacart, Customer Dashboards, Company Retail where _d_ = 50 is used and Million Song, where _d_ = 100 is used. We perform MAP inference by running our algorithms on the learned _**B** ,_ _**C** ,_ and _**V**_ . For all the following results, we set _α_ = 1 _._ 1 and _k_ = 8.

MAP Inference results for a variety of different datasets are provided in Figure 1. Surprisingly, the solution quality of our online algorithms compare favorably with the offline greedy algorithm while using only a single-pass over the data, and a tiny fraction of memory. In most cases, Online2-neighbor (Algorithm 3) performs better than Online-LSS (Algorithm 2) which in turn performs better than the online greedy algorithm (Algorithm 5). Strikingly, our online-2-neighbor algorithm performs even better than offline greedy in many cases. Furthermore, in some cases the other online algorithms also perform better than the offline greedy algorithm.

We also perform several experiments comparing the number of determinant computations (as a system-independent proxy for time) and the number of swaps (as a measure of solution consistency) of all our online algorithms. Results for determinant computations (Figure 3) and swaps (Figure 4) can be found in Appendix F. We summarize the main findings here. The number of determinant computations of Online-LSS is comparable to that of Online Greedy but the number of swaps performed is significantly smaller. Online-2-neighbor is the most time-consuming but superior performing algorithm in terms of solution quality.

We also investigate the performance of our algorithms under the random stream paradigm, where we consider a random permutation of some of the datasets used earlier. Results for the solution quality (Figure 5), number of determinant computations and swaps (Figure 6) can be found in Appendix F.3. In this setting, we see that Online-LSS and Online-2-neighbor have nearly identical performance and are always better than Online-Greedy in terms of solution quality and number of swaps.

We study the effect of varying _α_ in Online-LSS (Algorithm 2) for various values of set sizes _k_ in Appendices F.4 and F.5. We notice that, in general, the solution quality, number of determinant computations, and the number of swaps increase as _α_ decreases (Figure 7). We also see that as _k_ increases, the solution value decreases across all values of _α_ (Figure 8). This is in accordance with our intuition that the probability of larger sets should be smaller.

## 8 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we formulate and study the streaming and online MAP inference and learning problems for Nonsymmetric Determinantal Point Processes. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to study these problems in these practical settings. We design new algorithms for these problems, prove theoretical guarantees for them in terms of space required, time taken, and solution quality for our algorithms, and empirically show that they perform comparably or (sometimes) even better than state-of-the-art offline algorithms. We believe our work opens up completely new avenues for practical application of NDPPs and can lead to deployment of NDPPs in many more real-world settings.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper makes a compelling and well-motivated contribution by extending Nonsymmetric Determinantal Point Processes (NDPPs) into the online and streaming paradigms—a critical gap given the memory and latency constraints of real-world recommendation and summarization tasks. The introduction is clear, effectively positioning the work against existing symmetric DPP literature and establishing the necessity of the proposed single-pass, sub-linear memory algorithms. The summary table of theoretical bounds is a high-quality addition that allows for rapid assessment of the authors' claims. However, the most significant risk in this work lies in the **empirical rigor of the "comparable performance" claim**. While the paper argues these restricted algorithms perform as well as offline counterparts, the reviewer will need to see extensive evidence that these findings hold across diverse, high-variance datasets rather than just a few benchmarks. Furthermore, the mathematical complexity of maintaining a "valid solution" in an online setting for non-symmetric kernels is significant; the authors must ensure that the transition from static offline optimization to streaming heuristics does not inadvertently ignore convergence issues or stability constraints that typically arise in non-convex or non-symmetric learning scenarios.

comments.title: Possible indexing error in Algorithm 1 loop update
comments.quote: 5: i ← ⌈tk / n⌉
comments.explanation: In Section 4.2 (and the snippet in Section 4.1), the authors state that the goal is a k-size subset where the stream is partitioned into k segments of size n/k. For the i-th partition, indices usually range from (i-1)*(n/k) + 1 to i*(n/k). If t is the current timestep (1 to n), the formula ⌈tk / n⌉ attempts to map the current time to the partition index i ∈ {1, ..., k}. However, if t approaches n, the value i reaches k. If n is not perfectly divisible by k, the 'multiple of n/k' check is ill-defined. While usually common in streaming to assume n/k is an integer, the formulation should explicitly handle the partitioning boundaries for non-integral n/k to avoid index out-of-bounds or missed elements.

comments.quote: if S is the solution output by Algorithm 1 at the end of the stream and σ_min > 1
comments.explanation: Theorem 2 refers to a condition 'σ_min > 1' where σ_min is the smallest singular value of L_S. This condition is critical, yet the paper fails to specify whether this is a requirement for the algorithm to function, a constraint on the class of NDPPs, or a condition for a specific approximation ratio. Without contextual grounding or a citation characterizing the behavior of the determinant for matrices with σ_min <= 1, this condition appears arbitrary and potentially exclusionary for valid NDPP kernels.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper addresses a timely and significant gap by introducing the first online and streaming algorithms for MAP inference and learning in Nonsymmetric Determinantal Point Processes (NDPPs), effectively overcoming the memory and multi-pass limitations of existing offline methods. The motivation is compelling, particularly the utility of modeling positive correlations in dynamic, large-scale scenarios, and the introduction clearly distinguishes the streaming and online constraints while making strong claims regarding solution quality and memory efficiency. The writing is generally clear, and the formulation of maintaining a valid solution in the online setting is a noteworthy technical consideration. However, the proposal suffers from several high-level issues. First, the validity of comparisons with state-of-the-art offline algorithms requires rigorous justification; claiming comparable MAP quality and log-likelihood without accounting for the offline algorithms' ability to re-optimize over the full dataset risks overstatement, and the evaluation protocol must be scrutinized for fairness. Second, the complexity analysis in Table 1 utilizes parameters ($k, d, \Delta, \alpha$) that are not immediately defined in the text, and formatting artifacts in the table suggest potential presentation issues. Third, the claim of "significantly faster convergence" for the online learning algorithm is ambiguous given the single-pass constraint; it must be clarified whether this refers to convergence with respect to computational cost per update, final model quality, or effective sample utilization. Finally, the theoretical treatment of maintaining NDPP validity (e.g., kernel positive semi-definiteness or valid determinant structure) under sub-linear memory updates is a non-trivial challenge that appears glossed over in the high-level summary and warrants deeper explanation.

comments.title: Definition of Δ includes an unexplained division by k
comments.quote: \Delta := \frac{[\text{OPT}]_k}{\text{val}_{\text{nz}} k}
comments.explanation: I initially thought the ratio \Delta was simply meant to capture the condition number or approximation gap between the optimal solution and the baseline greedy value without swaps. Dividing by \text{val}_{\text{nz}} k is highly non-standard in online submodular/DPP literature, where \Delta is typically defined as the direct ratio [\text{OPT}]_k / \text{val}_{\text{nz}}. The extra factor of k changes the logarithmic term in the time bounds by an additive \log k, which is usually absorbed in big-O, but its explicit inclusion in the definition is confusing and likely a typographical error. I checked the surrounding text for justification but found none. If intentional, the authors should clarify why the baseline is normalized by k, otherwise it undermines the precise theoretical statement.

comments.title: Parameter constraint α ≥ 1 contradicts logarithmic space/time bounds
comments.quote: constant \alpha \ge 1
comments.explanation: While reading Algorithm 2 and 3 inputs, I noted the constraint \alpha \ge 1. However, the space and update time complexities explicitly feature \log_\alpha(\Delta). The logarithm with base \alpha is mathematically undefined (or infinite) when \alpha = 1. Online local search algorithms that maintain a stash for potential swaps strictly require a multiplicative improvement factor \alpha > 1 to guarantee a logarithmic number of swaps and thus the stated \log_\alpha(\Delta) space bound. This is a clear notation oversight; the authors almost certainly intended \alpha > 1.

comments.title: Ambiguous maximization expression in Algorithm 3 line 8
comments.quote: _ \{i, j\} \leftarrow \arg \max _{a,b\in S_} ( _f_ ( _S \cup \{t\} \ \setminus \{a\} ) _, _f_ ( _S \cup \{t −_ 1 _, t\} \ \setminus \{a, b\} ) )
comments.explanation: This line attempts to find the best swap(s) by comparing single-item swaps with the new point t and two-item swaps with {t-1, t}. However, applying \arg \max to a tuple of two function values `(f(...), f(...))` is syntactically and mathematically ambiguous. It is unclear whether the algorithm maximizes the first expression, the second, or takes the maximum over the union of all candidate swap values. A careful reader could implement this incorrectly by only considering single swaps or misinterpreting the tuple. The intent is likely to maximize over all valid candidate swap values, but the notation lacks the necessary set notation or scalarization to be reproducible.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for stream length (n vs m)
comments.quote: memory that is constant in _m_ , the number of baskets in the training data (or equivalently the length of the stream).
comments.explanation: In Section 4.1 and throughout the streaming/inference sections, the authors consistently use n to denote the length of the data stream. However, in the Introduction's summary of contributions, they switch to m when discussing the online learning problem, calling it 'the number of baskets in the training data (or equivalently the length of the stream)'. While the distinction might be semantically meaningful between inference and learning phases, reusing the concept of 'stream length' with two different variables without early unification or explicit cross-referencing creates unnecessary cognitive load and risks confusion when comparing algorithm complexities across sections. A brief clarifying sentence or unified notation would improve readability.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 Initialization Typo
comments.explanation: The pseudocode initializes only `s_0`, but the algorithm processes `k` partitions and requires maintaining a running candidate `s_i` for each partition `i \in [k]`. Line 6 references `s_i`, which would be undefined for `i > 0` under the given initialization. The algorithm should either initialize a loop over all `i \in [k]` or use an array/dictionary for `s`. This is a clear syntactic/logical error that prevents direct reproduction.

comments.title: Ambiguous Optimization Target in Algorithm 3
comments.explanation: The `arg max` is applied to a tuple comparing a 1-swap improvement with the current stream item `t` and a 2-swap improvement involving the previous stream item `t-1` and `t`. The notation does not specify how the algorithm extracts the candidate indices `{i, j}` from this comparison, nor how it handles cases where the best improvement is a 1-swap versus a 2-swap (e.g., different cardinalities of `{i, j}` or tie-breaking). A rigorous definition would take the maximum over the union of all valid candidate swaps, or explicitly branch based on swap cardinality and return the corresponding indices.

comments.title: OCR Corruption Obscuring Amortized Bounds and \Delta Definition

comments.title: Notation collision for parameter α across inference and learning sections
comments.quote:
increases _f_ ( _S_ ) at-least by a factor of _α ≥_ 1 where _α_ is a parameter to be chosen
regularizer R_ ( _**V** ,_ _**B**_ ) = _α_[] _[n] i_ =1 _µ_ _i[∥]_ _**[v]**_ _i[∥]_ 2[2][+] _[ β]_ ... where _α, β >_ 0 _are tunable hyperparameters._

comments.title: Contradiction in reported space complexity for Algorithm 4
comments.quote:
Table 1 lists Space for ONLINE-NDPP(Alg.4) as _d′_2 +_d_2
Theorem 9 states: Algorithm 4 uses O( _d[′]_[2] + _d_[2] ) space
The following paragraph states: It uses total space _O_ ( _nd_ + _d[′]_[2] ), which is independent of the stream length... > †Note that the _O_ ( _nd_ + _d_ 2) required to store the matrices _**V**_ , _**B**_ , and _**C**_ are omitted for simplicity

comments.title: Undefined variables in offline baseline complexity critique
comments.quote: Finally, it also incurs an update time of _O_ ( _pnd_[2] + _ptd[′]_[3] ), which is too much for the online setting.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper presents a timely and well-motivated contribution by introducing online and streaming formulations for MAP inference and learning in Nonsymmetric Determinantal Point Processes (NDPPs), an area that has been largely unexplored despite the growing importance of NDPPs in machine learning. The writing is generally clear and the contributions are explicitly enumerated, with a useful summary table providing an overview of the proposed algorithms and their complexities. However, the paper suffers from several significant issues. **First, the technical depth in the provided excerpt is insufficient**—the introduction and related work sections are thorough, but the paper as presented does not include any algorithmic details, theoretical guarantees, or experimental results beyond vague statements of "comparable performance." Without seeing the actual algorithms (e.g., STREAM-PARTITION, ONLINE-LSS) and their analysis, it is impossible to assess the novelty or validity of the claimed contributions. **Second, the paper lacks explicit formal definitions of the online and streaming settings** for NDPPs, such as precise objective functions, feasibility constraints, or performance metrics (e.g., regret or approximation ratios). **Third, the related work section is somewhat disconnected**, failing to clearly articulate how the proposed methods differ from or improve upon the cited offline methods beyond the obvious memory and pass constraints. **Finally, the paper would benefit from a more critical discussion of limitations**, such as the assumptions on data arrival order or the practical implications of maintaining a "valid solution at any point in time." Overall, while the problem formulation is promising, the manuscript in its current form does not provide enough substance to evaluate its scientific rigor or practical impact.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 partition finalization condition invalid for non-integer n/k
comments.quote: if t is a multiple of n/k then
comments.explanation: The algorithm uses the condition 'if t is a multiple of n/k' to finalize a partition and add the stored candidate s_i to the solution. However, n/k may not be an integer, so the condition will never be true for integer t. This would cause the algorithm to never add any element to S after initialization, making the algorithm incorrect for general n. The pseudocode does not specify any rounding or alternative condition, though the earlier description suggests partitions of size exactly n/k. This is a critical algorithmic flaw.

comments.title: Algorithm 3 step 8-9 ambiguous selection between single and double swap
comments.quote: i, j} ← arg max_{a,b∈S} ( f( S ∪ {t} \ {a} ), f( S ∪ {t − 1, t} \ {a, b} ))
comments.explanation: The notation is unclear: the arg max appears to take the maximum over two different expressions, but it does not specify how the choice between the single swap and the double swap is made. The two expressions have different numbers of variables (a vs a,b) and different sizes (|S| stays same for single swap, but double swap replaces two items). Furthermore, the double swap only considers the two most recent items (t-1,t) as the incoming set, which seems arbitrary and is not justified. The subsequent step 10 references 'if f_max > α·f(S)' and then a conditional 'if two items are chosen to be replaced', but the decision mechanism is not properly defined. This underspecification makes the algorithm difficult to implement correctly.

comments.title: Table 1 log2/log3 notation likely inconsistent with theorem statements
comments.quote: T_det(k,d)·k_log2(∆)  ...  T_det(k,d)·k^2 log3(∆)
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the update time expressions for online algorithms are written as 'k_log2(∆)' and 'k^2 log3(∆)', which could be interpreted as log base 2 and log base 3, respectively. However, Theorems 5 and 7 state worst-case update times as O(T_det(k,d)·k log^2(∆)) and O(T_det(k,d)·k^2 log^3(∆)), which use squared and cubed logarithms. The table entries are almost certainly meant to match those theorem statements, but the missing carets and presence of 'log2' and 'log3' create a potential misunderstanding. This is a formatting/OCR artifact, but it could mislead a reader who relies solely on the table.

comments.title: Garbled normalization constant equation
comments.quote: since we know that[] S⊆ [n] det( **L** S ) = det( **L** S ) det( **L** + **I** n ) (Kulesza & Taskar, 2012, Theorem 2.1).
comments.explanation: The equation is corrupted by OCR. The intended formula is the sum over all subsets S of det(L_S) equals det(L+I), not the nonsensical equality shown. Although the correct expression Pr[S] = det(L_S)/det(L+I_n) immediately follows, the garbled line could confuse a reader unfamiliar with the standard identity. The error is clearly an OCR artifact, but it remains a technical inaccuracy in the text.

comments.title: Uninitialized candidate variables in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: The algorithm initializes only s_0, but the loop uses s_i for i ≥ 1. When the first element of a new partition arrives (i newly computed), s_i has never been assigned a value. The comparison f(S_{i-1} ∪ {s_i}) is thus undefined, making the pseudocode non-executable as written. This is not a notation error but a missing initialization step; the algorithm would fail without a sentinel or an explicit initial assignment. I initially thought this might be resolved by assuming s_i starts as ∅, but f(S ∪ {∅}) is not meaningful. The intended behavior is likely that s_i is set to the first element seen in each partition, but the pseudocode does not specify this. This is a real bug.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Algorithm 2: handling of items with zero determinant before full set
comments.explanation: The pseudocode enters the else branch when either |S| >= k or (|S| < k and f(S ∪ {t}) == 0). In the latter case, S is not yet full, but the else block attempts a single-element swap, which does not increase the cardinality. The algorithm might then never reach size k if many items have zero determinants. The outline of Algorithm 2 states that after t ≥ k the algorithm maintains a solution of cardinality k, which implicitly assumes that the set reaches size k after k non-zero additions. The pseudocode does not handle the zero-determinant case before reaching size k, potentially leaving the implementation underspecified. A clearer approach would be to simply ignore items that give zero determinant until the set becomes full.

comments.title: Inconsistent space complexity between theorem and subsequent text
comments.quote: Theorem 9. Algorithm 4 uses O(d'^2 + d^2) space ... It uses total space O(nd + d'^2), which is independent of the stream length (and is therefore sub-linear in it as well);
comments.explanation: Theorem 9 states space O(d'^2 + d^2), with a footnote that the O(nd + d^2) needed for storing V, B, C is omitted. Immediately afterward the text claims 'It uses total space O(nd + d'^2)'. This is inconsistent: the theorem's omitted term includes both nd and d^2, but the text drops the d^2 term and presents a different total expression. A reader comparing the two statements will be confused about the actual space requirement.

comments.title: Regularizer uses global item frequencies unavailable in online setting
comments.quote: where µ_i is the number of occurrences of the item i in all the training data (subsets)
comments.explanation: The regularizer R(V,B) leverages µ_i, the total number of occurrences of item i over the entire training set. In an online, single-pass setting, the algorithm cannot know these counts for items that have not yet appeared or for items whose future appearances are unknown. The definition as written implies knowledge of the full dataset, contradicting the constraints of online learning (single pass, sublinear memory). It may be that the authors intended to use the count up to the current time step, but the text says 'in all the training data' and does not clarify any modification for the online setting. This is a fundamental flaw in the problem formulation.

comments.title: Optimization step in Algorithm 4 underspecified
comments.quote: Update V_{S_t}, B_{S_t}, C using ∇_V_{S_t} ψ_t, ∇_B_{S_t} ψ_t, ∇_C ψ_t (Eqs. 9, 10, 11) respectively.
comments.explanation: The algorithm describes an update using gradients, but does not specify the learning rate, the optimizer (e.g., stochastic gradient ascent with fixed or adaptive step size), or how the gradients are combined. Without these details, the method cannot be reproduced. While an appendix may provide more details, a self-contained algorithm description in the main text should at least mention that a gradient step with a given learning rate is taken.

comments.title: Ambiguous return statement in Algorithm 4
comments.quote: 6: return low-rank NDPP kernel L^{(t)} at time t
comments.explanation: The return statement is inside the while loop, suggesting that after processing each arriving set S_t the algorithm outputs the kernel. Typically, an online algorithm would output or record the model at each time step, but the phrasing 'return' is more natural for a final output after the loop terminates. The placement is ambiguous and could mislead a reader about the intended behavior.

comments.title: Garbled regularizer notation may obscure intended formula
comments.explanation: The OCR has introduced junk characters (e.g., '[]', misplaced brackets) making the formula hard to parse. The intended expression is almost certainly α∑_{i=1}^n (1/μ_i)‖v_i‖² + β∑_{i=1}^n (1/μ_i)‖b_i‖², but the current rendering could be misinterpreted (e.g., 'µ_1_i' might be read as μ_{1i} instead of 1/μ_i). Although automatic corrections were applied, the result remains unclear. Combined with the issue that μ_i is defined as the global count, this adds further confusion.

comments.title: Overclaiming theoretical guarantees for solution quality of online algorithms
comments.quote: prove theoretical guarantees for them in terms of space required, time taken, and solution quality for our algorithms
comments.explanation: The conclusion states that the paper proves theoretical guarantees for solution quality for their algorithms. However, from the provided context, only Algorithm 1 (Streaming Partition Greedy) has a solution quality guarantee in Theorem 2 (incomplete). The online algorithms (Algorithms 2 and 3) are only given time and space guarantees (Theorems 5 and 7). No solution quality guarantee is presented for them in the context. While it is possible that such guarantees are given in the appendix not shown, the conclusion as written implies that all algorithms have solution quality guarantees, which is misleading based on the provided evidence.

comments.title: Inaccurate intuition about probability of larger sets
comments.quote: We also see that as _k_ increases, the solution value decreases across all values of _α_ (Figure 8). This is in accordance with our intuition that the probability of larger sets should be smaller.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the observed decrease in solution value (determinant of L_S) as k increases is consistent with the intuition that the probability of larger sets should be smaller. However, in a k-DPP conditioning on cardinality k, the probability of a set of size k is proportional to det(L_S). There is no inherent monotonic relationship between k and the maximum possible det(L_S); it depends on the eigenvalues of L. For example, if L has eigenvalues >1, determinants of larger submatrices can increase. The intuition that 'probability of larger sets should be smaller' is not generally true for the unnormalized determinant values; it may hold for normalized probabilities across different k only if the normalization constant is accounted for, but the comparison is between different k values. The justification is weak and potentially misleading.

progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive Original (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive_original__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper presents a timely and well-motivated contribution by introducing online and streaming formulations for MAP inference and learning in Nonsymmetric Determinantal Point Processes (NDPPs), an area that has been largely unexplored despite the growing importance of NDPPs in machine learning. The writing is generally clear and the contributions are explicitly enumerated, with a useful summary table providing an overview of the proposed algorithms and their complexities. However, the paper suffers from several significant issues. **First, the technical depth in the provided excerpt is insufficient**—the introduction and related work sections are thorough, but the paper as presented does not include any algorithmic details, theoretical guarantees, or experimental results beyond vague statements of "comparable performance." Without seeing the actual algorithms (e.g., STREAM-PARTITION, ONLINE-LSS) and their analysis, it is impossible to assess the novelty or validity of the claimed contributions. **Second, the paper lacks explicit formal definitions of the online and streaming settings** for NDPPs, such as precise objective functions, feasibility constraints, or performance metrics (e.g., regret or approximation ratios). **Third, the related work section is somewhat disconnected**, failing to clearly articulate how the proposed methods differ from or improve upon the cited offline methods beyond the obvious memory and pass constraints. **Finally, the paper would benefit from a more critical discussion of limitations**, such as the assumptions on data arrival order or the practical implications of maintaining a "valid solution at any point in time." Overall, while the problem formulation is promising, the manuscript in its current form does not provide enough substance to evaluate its scientific rigor or practical impact.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 partition finalization condition invalid for non-integer n/k
comments.quote: if t is a multiple of n/k then
comments.explanation: The algorithm uses the condition 'if t is a multiple of n/k' to finalize a partition and add the stored candidate s_i to the solution. However, n/k may not be an integer, so the condition will never be true for integer t. This would cause the algorithm to never add any element to S after initialization, making the algorithm incorrect for general n. The pseudocode does not specify any rounding or alternative condition, though the earlier description suggests partitions of size exactly n/k. This is a critical algorithmic flaw.

comments.title: Algorithm 3 step 8-9 ambiguous selection between single and double swap
comments.quote: i, j} ← arg max_{a,b∈S} ( f( S ∪ {t} \ {a} ), f( S ∪ {t − 1, t} \ {a, b} ))
comments.explanation: The notation is unclear: the arg max appears to take the maximum over two different expressions, but it does not specify how the choice between the single swap and the double swap is made. The two expressions have different numbers of variables (a vs a,b) and different sizes (|S| stays same for single swap, but double swap replaces two items). Furthermore, the double swap only considers the two most recent items (t-1,t) as the incoming set, which seems arbitrary and is not justified. The subsequent step 10 references 'if f_max > α·f(S)' and then a conditional 'if two items are chosen to be replaced', but the decision mechanism is not properly defined. This underspecification makes the algorithm difficult to implement correctly.

comments.title: Table 1 log2/log3 notation likely inconsistent with theorem statements
comments.quote: T_det(k,d)·k_log2(∆)  ...  T_det(k,d)·k^2 log3(∆)
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the update time expressions for online algorithms are written as 'k_log2(∆)' and 'k^2 log3(∆)', which could be interpreted as log base 2 and log base 3, respectively. However, Theorems 5 and 7 state worst-case update times as O(T_det(k,d)·k log^2(∆)) and O(T_det(k,d)·k^2 log^3(∆)), which use squared and cubed logarithms. The table entries are almost certainly meant to match those theorem statements, but the missing carets and presence of 'log2' and 'log3' create a potential misunderstanding. This is a formatting/OCR artifact, but it could mislead a reader who relies solely on the table.

comments.title: Garbled normalization constant equation
comments.quote: since we know that[] S⊆ [n] det( **L** S ) = det( **L** S ) det( **L** + **I** n ) (Kulesza & Taskar, 2012, Theorem 2.1).
comments.explanation: The equation is corrupted by OCR. The intended formula is the sum over all subsets S of det(L_S) equals det(L+I), not the nonsensical equality shown. Although the correct expression Pr[S] = det(L_S)/det(L+I_n) immediately follows, the garbled line could confuse a reader unfamiliar with the standard identity. The error is clearly an OCR artifact, but it remains a technical inaccuracy in the text.

comments.title: Uninitialized candidate variables in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: The algorithm initializes only s_0, but the loop uses s_i for i ≥ 1. When the first element of a new partition arrives (i newly computed), s_i has never been assigned a value. The comparison f(S_{i-1} ∪ {s_i}) is thus undefined, making the pseudocode non-executable as written. This is not a notation error but a missing initialization step; the algorithm would fail without a sentinel or an explicit initial assignment. I initially thought this might be resolved by assuming s_i starts as ∅, but f(S ∪ {∅}) is not meaningful. The intended behavior is likely that s_i is set to the first element seen in each partition, but the pseudocode does not specify this. This is a real bug.

comments.title: Ambiguous partition boundary condition
comments.quote: 8: **if** _t_ is a multiple of _[n] k_ **[then]**
comments.explanation: The condition 't is a multiple of n/k' is unclear when n is not divisible by k. For example, n=100, k=3 gives n/k≈33.33; 'multiple of n/k' is not an integer. The algorithm presumably expects n to be divisible by k or uses floor/ceil partitioning, but the pseudocode does not clarify. This could lead to off-by-one errors in an implementation. It is a minor specification gap.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Algorithm 2: handling of items with zero determinant before full set
comments.explanation: The pseudocode enters the else branch when either |S| >= k or (|S| < k and f(S ∪ {t}) == 0). In the latter case, S is not yet full, but the else block attempts a single-element swap, which does not increase the cardinality. The algorithm might then never reach size k if many items have zero determinants. The outline of Algorithm 2 states that after t ≥ k the algorithm maintains a solution of cardinality k, which implicitly assumes that the set reaches size k after k non-zero additions. The pseudocode does not handle the zero-determinant case before reaching size k, potentially leaving the implementation underspecified. A clearer approach would be to simply ignore items that give zero determinant until the set becomes full.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Algorithm 3: distinguishing single vs double swap
comments.quote:
8: {i, j} ← arg max_{a,b∈S} ( f(S ∪ {t} \ {a}), f(S ∪ {t-1, t} \ {a,b}) )
9: fmax ← max_{a,b∈S} ( f(S ∪ {t} \ {a}), f(S ∪ {t-1, t} \ {a,b}) )
10: if fmax > α · f(S) then
11: if two items are chosen to be replaced: then
12: S ← S ∪ {t-1, t} \ {i, j}
...
15: else
16: S ← S ∪ {t} \ {i}
comments.explanation: Line 8 tries to return a pair (i,j) even when the best improvement comes from a single-element swap. The pseudocode does not specify how to determine which case produced the maximum in line 9. A reader cannot tell whether the best improvement was from a single swap (involving only a) or a double swap (involving both a and b). The condition on line 11 (“if two items are chosen to be replaced”) is thus not decidable from the given expression. The text mentions that the algorithm checks both possibilities, but the formal description is incomplete. The reference to Appendix B may resolve this, but in the main text it remains ambiguous.

comments.title: Inconsistent space complexity between theorem and subsequent text
comments.quote: Theorem 9. Algorithm 4 uses O(d'^2 + d^2) space ... It uses total space O(nd + d'^2), which is independent of the stream length (and is therefore sub-linear in it as well);
comments.explanation: Theorem 9 states space O(d'^2 + d^2), with a footnote that the O(nd + d^2) needed for storing V, B, C is omitted. Immediately afterward the text claims 'It uses total space O(nd + d'^2)'. This is inconsistent: the theorem's omitted term includes both nd and d^2, but the text drops the d^2 term and presents a different total expression. A reader comparing the two statements will be confused about the actual space requirement.

comments.title: Regularizer uses global item frequencies unavailable in online setting
comments.quote: where µ_i is the number of occurrences of the item i in all the training data (subsets)
comments.explanation: The regularizer R(V,B) leverages µ_i, the total number of occurrences of item i over the entire training set. In an online, single-pass setting, the algorithm cannot know these counts for items that have not yet appeared or for items whose future appearances are unknown. The definition as written implies knowledge of the full dataset, contradicting the constraints of online learning (single pass, sublinear memory). It may be that the authors intended to use the count up to the current time step, but the text says 'in all the training data' and does not clarify any modification for the online setting. This is a fundamental flaw in the problem formulation.

comments.title: Optimization step in Algorithm 4 underspecified
comments.quote: Update V_{S_t}, B_{S_t}, C using ∇_V_{S_t} ψ_t, ∇_B_{S_t} ψ_t, ∇_C ψ_t (Eqs. 9, 10, 11) respectively.
comments.explanation: The algorithm describes an update using gradients, but does not specify the learning rate, the optimizer (e.g., stochastic gradient ascent with fixed or adaptive step size), or how the gradients are combined. Without these details, the method cannot be reproduced. While an appendix may provide more details, a self-contained algorithm description in the main text should at least mention that a gradient step with a given learning rate is taken.

comments.title: Ambiguous return statement in Algorithm 4
comments.quote: 6: return low-rank NDPP kernel L^{(t)} at time t
comments.explanation: The return statement is inside the while loop, suggesting that after processing each arriving set S_t the algorithm outputs the kernel. Typically, an online algorithm would output or record the model at each time step, but the phrasing 'return' is more natural for a final output after the loop terminates. The placement is ambiguous and could mislead a reader about the intended behavior.

comments.title: Garbled regularizer notation may obscure intended formula
comments.explanation: The OCR has introduced junk characters (e.g., '[]', misplaced brackets) making the formula hard to parse. The intended expression is almost certainly α∑_{i=1}^n (1/μ_i)‖v_i‖² + β∑_{i=1}^n (1/μ_i)‖b_i‖², but the current rendering could be misinterpreted (e.g., 'µ_1_i' might be read as μ_{1i} instead of 1/μ_i). Although automatic corrections were applied, the result remains unclear. Combined with the issue that μ_i is defined as the global count, this adds further confusion.

comments.title: Overclaiming theoretical guarantees for solution quality of online algorithms
comments.quote: prove theoretical guarantees for them in terms of space required, time taken, and solution quality for our algorithms
comments.explanation: The conclusion states that the paper proves theoretical guarantees for solution quality for their algorithms. However, from the provided context, only Algorithm 1 (Streaming Partition Greedy) has a solution quality guarantee in Theorem 2 (incomplete). The online algorithms (Algorithms 2 and 3) are only given time and space guarantees (Theorems 5 and 7). No solution quality guarantee is presented for them in the context. While it is possible that such guarantees are given in the appendix not shown, the conclusion as written implies that all algorithms have solution quality guarantees, which is misleading based on the provided evidence.

comments.title: Inaccurate intuition about probability of larger sets
comments.quote: We also see that as _k_ increases, the solution value decreases across all values of _α_ (Figure 8). This is in accordance with our intuition that the probability of larger sets should be smaller.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the observed decrease in solution value (determinant of L_S) as k increases is consistent with the intuition that the probability of larger sets should be smaller. However, in a k-DPP conditioning on cardinality k, the probability of a set of size k is proportional to det(L_S). There is no inherent monotonic relationship between k and the maximum possible det(L_S); it depends on the eigenvalues of L. For example, if L has eigenvalues >1, determinants of larger submatrices can increase. The intuition that 'probability of larger sets should be smaller' is not generally true for the unnormalized determinant values; it may hold for normalized probabilities across different k only if the normalization constant is accounted for, but the comparison is between different k values. The justification is weak and potentially misleading.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a critical practical gap in the machine learning community by formulating and solving the first online and streaming inference and learning problems for Nonsymmetric Determinantal Point Processes (NDPPs). The introduction is clear and well-motivated, convincingly arguing that while NDPPs offer greater expressiveness for modeling positive item correlations—unlike their symmetric counterparts—the current state-of-the-art algorithms are too memory-intensive for large-scale and real-time applications. The distinction between the streaming and online settings (validity at every step) is clearly defined, and the contributions are well-summarized in the abstract. The primary concern, however, lies in the theoretical implications of these constraints. Given the computational difficulty of MAP inference on NDPPs, the feedback should rigorously scrutinize the approximation guarantees and solution quality of the proposed single-pass algorithms to ensure they do not significantly sacrifice the offline optimum while adhering to the strict online and memory constraints.

comments.title: Table 1 inconsistency for ONLINE-GREEDY algorithm
comments.quote: ONLINE-GREEDY(Alg.5) _T_det(_k, d_)_·nk_ _T_det(_k, d_)_·nk_
comments.explanation: The table lists the same mathematical expression (`_T_det(_k, d_)_·nk_`) for both the 'Update Time' and 'Total Time' columns. Looking at the adjacent row for ONLINE-LSS, the 'Update Time' (e.g., `k log(Δ)`) and 'Total Time' (e.g., `nk + k log(Δ)`) follow a consistent pattern where the Total Time is the sum of updates scaled by the stream length `n`. For ONLINE-GREEDY, the current entry implies both update and total costs are `nk T_det`. This is mathematically impossible because the total time cannot be smaller than the sum of update times (unless updates are negative, which is not the case for time complexity). The 'Update Time' column likely intended to show `_T_det(_k, d_)_·k_` (updating by checking `k` candidates) and the 'Total Time' column `_T_det(_k, d_)_·_nk_` (summing `n` such updates).
